title: Ultrafast spectroscopy and role of interlayer coupling in high harmonic generation from layered solids
abstract: High harmonic generation (HHG) in solids has recently emerged as a powerful all-optical approach for probing material properties and ultrafast electron dynamics in quantum systems. It has been widely applied for studying two-dimensional and layered solids of various kinds. In these studies, the laser is usually polarized within the layered planes, where most electron dynamics occurs, while out-of-plane hopping is commonly neglected. This is despite of interlayer hopping being ubiquitous in nano-systems. Here we develop theory for HHG in layered solids in presence of interlayer coupling and employ it for studying strong-field driven hexagonal BN, graphite, and the transition metal dichalcogenide WS$_2$. We show that sufficiently intense couplings can alter typical HHG emission characteristics such as angular or ellipticity dependence even when the driving laser is polarized in-plane. We develop an analytic perturbation theory for the laser-driven current expanded in the interlayer coupling parameter and explicitly show that HHG yields follow a 4'th order polynomial form, which is validated numerically. Our work should motivate experiments for probing interlayer coupling via HHG spectroscopy, as well as exploring its modulation as a control parameter for ultrafast dynamics and attopulse generation via laser driving and mechanical strain.
summary: This paper explores how the vertical coupling between layers in materials like graphite and hBN influences high harmonic generation. The authors demonstrate that even when a laser is polarized in-plane, the out-of-plane hopping can change the direction and shape of the emitted harmonics. They provide a new mathematical framework using a 4th-order polynomial to accurately predict these changes. This work suggests that HHG can be used as a powerful spectroscopic tool to measure interlayer interactions and potentially control ultrafast electron dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating how interlayer coupling affects high harmonic generation (HHG) in layered solids, specifically addressing the gap where out-of-plane hopping is often neglected.
main_result: The authors show that interlayer coupling can significantly alter HHG angular and ellipticity dependence and develop an analytic theory showing HHG yields follow a 4th-order polynomial in the coupling parameter.
method: Numerical solutions of the Semiconductor Bloch Equations (SBE) in the Houston basis and length gauge, combined with an analytic perturbative expansion of the laser-driven current.
model_or_system: A 4-band tight-binding model for AB-stacked honeycomb lattices, applied to materials including graphite, hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), and WS2, including interlayer hopping along the c-axis.
key_observables: HHG emission spectrum, time-dependent electric current, angular dependence of harmonics, and ellipticity dependence of harmonics.
important_parameters: Interlayer coupling strength (tz), laser polarization angle (theta), laser ellipticity (epsilon), and dephasing time (T2).
main_assumptions: The interlayer hopping is treated as a perturbation; the model uses a phenomenological dephasing time; the laser is assumed to be off-resonant and femtosecond-scale.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Lattice geometry, Brillouin zone, and band structures. Fig 2: HHG yields vs. orientation for graphite and hBN. Fig 3: HHG yields vs. ellipticity. Fig 4: Dependence of HHG yield on the interlayer hopping parameter with comparison of power-law and 4th-order polynomial fits.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem of interlayer coupling, description of the tight-binding model and SBE method, presentation of the analytic perturbative theory, numerical validation via HHG spectra and angular/ellipticity dependence, and discussion of implications for spectroscopy and control.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a–b) Illustration of lattice geometry showing top view over xy planes (a), and side view showing AB stacking configuration (b). (c) Hexagonal BZ with high symmetry points for kz = 0 plane. (d,e) TB band structures for graphene/graphite and monolayer/bulk hBN, where bands are indicated by green dashed lines for the monolayers, and black solid lines for bulks. (f–g) Exemplary HHG spectra for (f) graphene/graphite and (g) hBN, calculated for linear driving along Γ-K for various values of interlayer couplings (legends are in eV).

FIG. 2: Orientation dependence of normalized harmonic yields from layered solids for various interlayer coupling strengths. (a–f) Normalized HHG yields from graphite. (g–l) Normalized HHG yields from hBN. Select harmonics are presented that cover the full range of physical behaviors observed (all other harmonics orders are delegated to the SI).

FIG. 3: Ellipticity dependence of normalized harmonic yields from layered solids for varying interlayer coupling strength. (a–f) Select harmonics from graphite. (g–l) Select harmonics from hBN. Other harmonic orders, as well as inter/intra-band separation, is delegated to the SI.

FIG. 4: Harmonic yield dependence on the interlayer hopping parameter up to 100 meV in the perturbative regime. Exact numerical results from SBE simulations are given by the empty circle black markers. Black solid lines and blue dash lines are: generic power law fitted function, 4’th-order polynomial fitted function, respectively. Note that the R2 values are given in the legend for each fit, and the sub-text in R2 indicates the number of points over which the fit was made (generally the power-law dependence was fitted over a shorter domain since it cannot capture curvature changes that appear towards larger tz (see discussion in text)). (a–f) HHG in graphite. (g–l) HHG in hBN.
